candidates.title: Auditing SAE Feature Absorption: Hedging Dominance, Rate-Distortion Diagnostics, and the L0 Phase Transition on JumpReLU SAEs

collisions.paper: Chanin et al., 2024. A is for Absorption: Studying Feature Splitting and Absorption in Sparse Autoencoders. arXiv:2409.14507 (NeurIPS 2025 Oral)
collisions.overlap: Defines absorption, introduces the supervised absorption metric, studies first-letter spelling on GPT-2 Small and Gemma-2-2B with JumpReLU SAEs. Reports absorption rates across layers and SAE widths.
collisions.notes: Foundation paper. The proposed work directly audits the validity of the Chanin metric itself on JumpReLU SAEs. Chanin et al. use the metric to measure absorption but do not audit the metric's validity (shuffled controls, confound decomposition). They note JumpReLU is tested but do not validate metric thresholds for JumpReLU activation geometry. The key difference: Chanin et al. APPLY the metric; this work AUDITS the metric.

collisions.paper: Karvonen et al., 2025. SAEBench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Sparse Autoencoders. arXiv:2503.09532 (ICML 2025)
collisions.overlap: Includes Feature Absorption and RAVEL as evaluation metrics across 200+ SAEs. Reports inverse scaling of absorption with SAE size for JumpReLU/TopK. Explicitly notes absorption metric uses spelling only.
collisions.notes: SAEBench uses the Chanin metric as-is across many SAEs but does NOT validate the metric itself. Their finding that JumpReLU worsens absorption accepts the metric at face value. The proposed work's headline finding -- that shuffled controls exceed true labels -- directly challenges the interpretability of SAEBench's absorption rankings. SAEBench acknowledges 'not useful for evaluating domain-specific feature absorption.' The two works are complementary: SAEBench is large-scale application, this work is metric validation.

collisions.paper: Chanin et al., 2025. Feature Hedging: Correlated Features Break Narrow Sparse Autoencoders. arXiv:2505.11756
collisions.overlap: Identifies hedging as a complementary failure mode to absorption. Shows hedging-absorption tradeoff in Matryoshka SAEs. Provides theoretical analysis of hedging mechanism.
collisions.notes: Feature hedging is the conceptual complement to this work's hedging dominance finding. Chanin et al. (2025) define hedging theoretically and demonstrate it in narrow SAEs. The proposed work's confound decomposition (98.6% hedging at L0=22) empirically quantifies how much of standard 'absorption' measurements are actually hedging. This is the first quantitative decomposition separating the two. The hedging paper predicts this confound exists; the proposed work measures it.

collisions.paper: Chanin & Garriga-Alonso, 2025. Sparse but Wrong: Incorrect L0 Leads to Incorrect Features. arXiv:2508.16560
collisions.overlap: Shows that incorrect L0 causes SAEs to learn wrong features via hedging. Demonstrates that low L0 produces feature mixing that degrades interpretability. Proposes method to find correct L0.
collisions.notes: The 'Sparse but Wrong' paper establishes that L0 is a critical parameter for feature quality, which is consistent with the proposed L0 phase transition finding. Key difference: 'Sparse but Wrong' studies general feature quality (probing accuracy); the proposed work specifically maps the L0-absorption curve and identifies a phase transition in the 40-80 range. The proposed work also decomposes the mechanism (hedging vs. competitive exclusion) at each L0 point, which 'Sparse but Wrong' does not.

collisions.paper: Bussmann et al., 2025. Learning Multi-Level Features with Matryoshka Sparse Autoencoders. arXiv:2503.17547 (ICML 2025)
collisions.overlap: Shows Matryoshka SAEs reduce absorption on spelling. Uses nested dictionaries to prevent child features from suppressing parents.
collisions.notes: Matryoshka SAE is an architecture mitigation for absorption. The proposed work questions whether the metric used to evaluate the mitigation is valid for JumpReLU SAEs. If the metric does not transfer, then Matryoshka SAE's absorption claims on JumpReLU also require re-evaluation.

collisions.paper: Korznikov et al., 2025. OrtSAE: Orthogonal Sparse Autoencoders Uncover Atomic Features. arXiv:2509.22033
collisions.overlap: Reduces absorption by 65% via cosine similarity penalty. Demonstrates causal link between decoder geometry and absorption.
collisions.notes: Another architecture mitigation. Evaluated using the Chanin metric on spelling. The proposed metric audit finding implies OrtSAE's absorption reduction claims should be validated on the audited metric.

collisions.paper: Li et al., 2025. Time-Aware Feature Selection (ATM-SAE). arXiv:2510.08855
collisions.overlap: Reports absorption score of 0.0068 on Gemma-2-2B layer 12, outperforming JumpReLU (0.0114). Uses the Chanin metric methodology.
collisions.notes: ATM-SAE reports very low absorption using the Chanin metric. The proposed work's finding of universal control failure suggests ATM-SAE's absorption advantage may need re-evaluation with proper baselines.

collisions.paper: Tang et al., 2025. A Unified Theory of Sparse Dictionary Learning. arXiv:2512.05534
collisions.overlap: First theoretical framework explaining absorption and dead neurons as consequences of non-identifiability in SDL optimization. Proposes feature anchoring to restore identifiability.
collisions.notes: Theoretical framework that explains WHY absorption occurs. The proposed work is empirical: it audits the METRIC for measuring absorption, not the theoretical mechanism. Complementary perspectives.

collisions.paper: KronSAE (arXiv:2505.22255). Kronecker Factorization Improves SAE Efficiency and Interpretability.
collisions.overlap: Lower absorption score via compositional architecture. Evaluates on standard SAEBench spelling.
collisions.notes: Another architecture fix evaluated on the Chanin metric. Same re-evaluation implication as other mitigations.

collisions.paper: Tilde Research. The Rate Distortion Dance of Sparse Autoencoders. Blog post, 2025.
collisions.overlap: Connects SAE behavior to rate-distortion theory. Discusses power-laws in feature usage and sensitivity under sparsity sweeps.
collisions.notes: General rate-distortion framing of SAEs. The proposed CMI diagnostic draws on successive refinement theory specifically, connecting it to absorption susceptibility. The blog does not address absorption.

collisions.paper: Chanin & Garriga-Alonso, 2026. SynthSAEBench: Evaluating SAEs on Scalable Realistic Synthetic Data. arXiv:2602.14687
collisions.overlap: Synthetic benchmark including hierarchical features and absorption measurement with ground-truth access.
collisions.notes: Studies absorption on synthetic data with known ground truth. The proposed work audits absorption on real models (Gemma 2 2B) where ground truth is not known. Complementary approaches.

collisions.paper: Huang et al., 2024. RAVEL: Evaluating Interpretability Methods on Disentangling Language Model Representations. ACL 2024.
collisions.overlap: Provides city/country/continent/language hierarchy benchmark data.
collisions.notes: Data source for cross-domain experiments. RAVEL measures disentanglement, not absorption.

collisions.paper: Resurrecting the Salmon: Rethinking Mechanistic Interpretability with Domain-Specific SAEs. arXiv:2508.09363 (2025)
collisions.overlap: Notes SAEBench absorption metric is not useful for domain-specific evaluation. Proposes domain-specific SAE training.
collisions.notes: Identifies the same gap. Proposes a different solution (domain-specific training) vs the proposed work's solution (metric audit and cross-domain measurement).

candidates.differentiation_notes:
The novelty is strong across all three headline contributions:

1. METRIC AUDIT (no prior work): No published paper validates the Chanin absorption metric with shuffled-label controls on JumpReLU SAEs. The universal control failure (shuffled > true in all 5 domains) is a completely new finding. All prior work applies the metric at face value.

2. CONFOUND DECOMPOSITION (no prior work): The 98.6% hedging / 1.4% hierarchy-driven decomposition at L0=22 is the first quantitative separation of these two confounds. Feature Hedging (Chanin et al., 2025) defines hedging theoretically; Sparse but Wrong (Chanin & Garriga-Alonso, 2025) shows L0 effects on feature quality; neither quantifies the hedging fraction within standard absorption measurements.

3. L0 PHASE TRANSITION (partially novel): The L0-absorption monotonic decline is qualitatively expected from 'Sparse but Wrong,' but the specific phase transition in L0=40-80 with cross-layer stability (CV<10%) and the 42.9% to 0.8% quantitative mapping is new. No prior work maps this curve.

4. CMI DIAGNOSTIC (novel): The connection between conditional mutual information and absorption susceptibility via successive refinement theory is new. No prior work applies rate-distortion diagnostics to predict absorption.

5. CROSS-DOMAIN MEASUREMENTS (novel): First absorption measurements on knowledge hierarchies. The finding that all rates fall below shuffled controls is itself informative.

The main risk is not prior work collision but rather that the metric audit is a 'negative result' paper -- showing a metric does not work is less glamorous than proposing a new metric. However, the 4/7 falsified hypotheses and honest negative results have been consistently rated as the paper's strongest aspect.

candidates.title: Geometric Forensics of Feature Absorption: Unsupervised Detection via Decoder Weight Topology in Sparse Autoencoders

collisions.paper: Korznikov et al., 2025. OrtSAE: Orthogonal Sparse Autoencoders Uncover Atomic Features. arXiv:2509.22033
collisions.overlap: Uses decoder pairwise cosine similarity as the primary signal for identifying and reducing absorption. Density plots compare cosine similarity distributions between absorbed/non-absorbed features. Effectively a one-signal unsupervised detector.
collisions.notes: OrtSAE already demonstrates decoder cosine similarity as a strong predictor of absorption. The proposed multi-signal ensemble needs to demonstrate substantial improvement over this baseline.

collisions.paper: Li et al., 2025. The Geometry of Concepts: Sparse Autoencoder Feature Structure. Entropy.
collisions.overlap: Computes decoder Gram matrix, analyzes cosine similarity clustering, co-occurrence patterns, and spatial structure at multiple scales.
collisions.notes: Gram matrix analysis already exists. The proposed work's Gram matrix contribution overlaps significantly.

collisions.paper: Bussmann et al., 2025. Matryoshka SAEs. arXiv:2503.17547 (ICML 2025)
collisions.overlap: Uses maximum decoder cosine similarity as a disentanglement/absorption diagnostic.
collisions.notes: Decoder cosine similarity is already a standard diagnostic metric.

collisions.paper: KronSAE (arXiv:2505.22255)
collisions.overlap: Demonstrates geometric properties of structured decoder predict absorption behavior.
collisions.notes: KronSAE shows that structured decoder geometry correlates with absorption reduction.

collisions.paper: Evaluating and Designing SAEs by Approximating Quasi-Orthogonality (arXiv:2503.24277)
collisions.overlap: Proposes evaluation metrics beyond decoder cosine similarities that link feature activations to input representations.
collisions.notes: Overlaps with the proposed work's goal of going beyond simple cosine similarity for SAE evaluation.

candidates.differentiation_notes: The primary signal (decoder cosine similarity / Gram matrix) is well-explored by OrtSAE and The Geometry of Concepts. The proposed activation-decoder misalignment (ADM) and residual absorption score (RAS) appear novel, but the core Gram matrix analysis has significant overlap. To claim novelty, the work must: (1) demonstrate the multi-signal ensemble substantially outperforms single cosine similarity (the OrtSAE baseline), (2) show it works on non-spelling domains, (3) provide theoretical justification for additional signals. Consider repositioning as a component of cand_metric_audit rather than standalone.

candidates.title: Immunological Imprinting in Sparse Autoencoders: Cross-Reactive Absorption and Frequency-Ratio Scaling Laws

collisions.paper: Chanin et al., 2024. A is for Absorption. arXiv:2409.14507 (NeurIPS 2025 Oral)
collisions.overlap: Defines absorption for hierarchical features. Delta-absorption theory explains the sparsity-driven mechanism for parent-child pairs.
collisions.notes: Chanin et al. study ONLY hierarchical absorption. The immunological analogy predicts cross-reactive absorption between non-hierarchical co-occurring features -- a genuinely different prediction.

collisions.paper: Chanin et al., 2025. Feature Hedging. arXiv:2505.11756
collisions.overlap: Hedging affects correlated (potentially non-hierarchical) features. Hedging merges correlated features in narrow SAEs.
collisions.notes: CLOSEST prior work. Critical distinction: hedging is a WIDTH/CAPACITY problem (narrow SAEs merge features); cross-reactive absorption would be a SPARSITY problem (suppression even in sufficiently wide SAEs). If cross-reactive absorption in wide SAEs is empirically indistinguishable from hedging, novelty collapses.

collisions.paper: Tang et al., 2025. Unified Theory of SDL. arXiv:2512.05534
collisions.overlap: General theoretical framework that may subsume the mechanism as a special case of spurious optima.
collisions.notes: Does not make the specific cross-reactive prediction or use immunological framing.

candidates.differentiation_notes: The immunological imprinting analogy and cross-reactive absorption prediction are genuinely unprecedented in the SAE literature. No prior work predicts or tests for absorption-like suppression between semantically unrelated but co-occurring features. The immunological framing (Original Antigenic Sin) has zero precedent in ML interpretability. Main risk: distinction from feature hedging. If cross-reactive absorption is empirically indistinguishable from hedging, novelty reduces to 'hedging with a biological analogy.' The proposal should include explicit controls in wide SAEs where hedging is minimal.

candidates.title: Quantitative Theory of Feature Absorption: Coherence, Frequency, and the Absorption Phase Boundary

collisions.paper: Tang et al., 2025. A Unified Theory of Sparse Dictionary Learning. arXiv:2512.05534
collisions.overlap: First theoretical framework for absorption in SDL. Characterizes global solutions, non-identifiability, and spurious optima. Proposes feature anchoring as a principled fix.
collisions.notes: The unified theory provides a general framework that may subsume the proposed coherence-frequency formula as a special case. However, Tang et al. do not derive specific absorption probability formulas from decoder properties, nor do they predict a phase boundary. The proposed work is more narrow and predictive; Tang et al. are more general and structural.

collisions.paper: Korznikov et al., 2025. OrtSAE. arXiv:2509.22033
collisions.overlap: Establishes causal link between decoder cosine similarity (coherence) and absorption. Demonstrates that reducing cosine similarity reduces absorption by 65%.
collisions.notes: OrtSAE provides empirical evidence that coherence drives absorption. The proposed formula formalizes this into a quantitative prediction. The risk is that the formula adds limited predictive value beyond OrtSAE's empirical observation.

collisions.paper: Spielman et al., 2012. Exact Recovery of Sparsely-Used Dictionaries. COLT 2012.
collisions.overlap: Classical dictionary identifiability results. Establishes conditions under which sparse dictionaries can be recovered.
collisions.notes: The proposed critical width formula M_c >= K(1 + D*mu_avg^2)/(1-epsilon) builds on classical identifiability. This is appropriate prior art, not a collision.

collisions.paper: Chanin & Garriga-Alonso, 2025. Sparse but Wrong. arXiv:2508.16560
collisions.overlap: Shows L0 is a critical parameter. The proposed phase boundary prediction (absorption becomes negligible above a critical L0) is related.
collisions.notes: 'Sparse but Wrong' shows L0 matters for feature quality but does not derive an absorption phase boundary formula.

collisions.paper: Cui et al., 2025. Necessary and Sufficient Conditions for Identifiable SAEs.
collisions.overlap: Establishes identifiability conditions for tied-weight SAEs.
collisions.notes: Addresses identifiability, not absorption prediction. Complementary theoretical contribution.

candidates.differentiation_notes: The proposed quantitative formula is novel, but faces two risks: (1) The unified theory of SDL (Tang et al., 2025) provides a more principled theoretical framework that may render the proposed mean-field approximation obsolete; (2) OrtSAE already demonstrates the coherence-absorption link empirically. The formula's value depends on predictive accuracy. If the sigmoid model achieves R^2 > 0.5 and correctly predicts which feature pairs absorb, the contribution is meaningful. If R^2 < 0.3, the formula adds little beyond OrtSAE's empirical observation. The critical width prediction is the most distinctive element.

search_summary.search_coverage_note: Searches were conducted via WebSearch covering arXiv, Semantic Scholar, OpenReview, conference proceedings, and community forums through April 2026. No arXiv MCP or Google Scholar MCP were available; web search provided thorough coverage of the indexed literature.
